One light-day away means it will take the spacecraft 24 hours or more to send a signal to Earth.
November 18 is going to be a date to remember!
We at IFLScience have been excited about this date for a while.
On November 15, Voyager 1 will reach 25.9 billion kilometers (15 billion miles) away from Earth, a distance that takes light more than 24 hours to cover.
“Alpha Centauri is the closest star to our own right now, but because stars are moving, Voyager 1 will actually get within 1.7 light-years of AC +79 3888 (aka Gliese 445) in 40,000 years.”"},{"url":"https://www.cnn.com/2025/12/09/science/voyager-1-light-day-earth","title":"Voyager 1 will reach one light-day from Earth in 2026. Here’s what that means | CNN","content":"Voyager 1 will reach one light-day from Earth in 2026.
Voyager 1, NASA’s deep-space probe, could soon become the first spacecraft to reach a historic milestone.
Launched in 1977, Voyager 1 is the farthest spacecraft from our planet, currently exploring interstellar space 15.8 billion miles away.
Launched on a journey to explore Jupiter and Saturn, Voyager 1 has been traveling away from Earth on the same trajectory and at the same speed — 38,000 miles per hour — since its flyby of Saturn in November 1980.
Scientists are interested in understanding how the sun’s magnetic field changes and interacts at the heliopause, the boundary of the heliosphere where hot solar wind streaming from the sun meets cold interstellar space."},{"url":"https://www.popsci.com/science/voyager-one-light-day-earth/","title":"Voyager 1 is almost one light-day from Earth | Popular Science","content":"Nov 24, 2025 — On November 15, 2026, the spacecraft will officially be one light-day from Earth. travels at 186,000 miles per second. This adds up to about 5. ...
As far as physicists can tell, nothing moves faster than the speed of light in a vacuum, which travels at 186,000 miles per second.
This adds up to about 5.88 trillion miles every Julian calendar year (365.25 days).
In little less than a year, Voyager 1 will finally be 16.1 billion miles from Earth, equivalent to the same distance light travels in 24 hours."},{"url":"https://www.iflscience.com/on-november-13-2026-voyager-will-reach-one-full-light-day-away-from-earth-81432","title":"On November 13, 2026, A Human-Made Object Will Reach One Light-Day From The Earth For The First Time In History | IFLScience","content":"Nov 4, 2025 — On November 13, 2026, Voyager Will Reach One Full Light-Day Away From Earth … reach 25.9 billion kilometers (16 billion miles) from Earth, this ...
The fastest human spaceflight remains 39,937.7 kilometers per hour (24,816.1 miles per hour), and at those speeds, it would take 3,730 hours to travel just 1 astronomical unit (AU), the distance between the Earth and the Sun.
"The distant Oort cloud marks the gravitational edge of the Solar System, in a vast region of undiscovered objects," NASA explains of the Oort cloud, first hypothesized by Dutch astronomer Jan Oort in 1950.
"It will take about 300 years for Voyager 1 to reach the inner edge of the Oort Cloud and possibly about 30,000 years to fly beyond it."
A paper examining the future stellar flybys of probes on an escape velocity from the Solar System puts the figure at more like 44,000 years.
An earlier version of this article was published in June 2025."},{"url":"https://mezha.net/eng/bukvy/voyager-1-to-reach-one-light-day-distance-from-earth-in-2026/","title":"Voyager 1 to Reach One Light-Day Distance from Earth in 2026 | Ukraine news - #Mezha","content":"This explanation was provided by Susie Dodd, Voyager project manager at NASA’s Jet Propulsion Laboratory (JPL).
One light-day is about 16 billion miles (26 billion kilometers).
According to NASA, the approximate data transmission speed is about 160 bits per second; this means that data arrive slowly and the signal weakens with distance.
Voyager continues to measure these waves and interactions, helping shape the understanding of what the space environment beyond the Sun’s influence looks like."}]}
